common_name: Gulf Gold Peach (Low-Chill Early Yellow Peach)

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter (January–February in zone 9) in well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.5), fertile loam in full sun; Gulf Gold requires only 150 chill hours and is one of the earliest-ripening, most reliably productive peaches for zone 8–9.

directions: Plant bare-root trees in late winter in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile loam in full sun; Gulf Gold is a low-chill peach cultivar developed by the University of Florida — requiring only 150 chill hours (hours below 45°F) to break dormancy reliably, making it one of the most dependable peach varieties for the Gulf Coast where chill hours are consistently limited. The fruit is medium-sized with yellow flesh, excellent eating quality, and very good flavor for a low-chill peach variety — ripening in late May to early June in zone 9, well ahead of standard-chill peaches that struggle to fruit in the Gulf South. Gulf Gold is a white-flowering, self-fertile peach that does not require a pollinizer, though planting two low-chill varieties together improves yield. Prune annually after harvest to maintain an open-center tree structure at 10–12 feet — reducing disease pressure and making fruit easier to harvest. Peach leaf curl, brown rot, and bacterial spot are the primary disease challenges in humid Gulf South climates; copper fungicide applied at dormant bud swell and follow-up fungicide applications through bloom are essential for reliable production. Thin fruit to 6 inches apart after natural fruit drop (June drop) for full-sized, high-quality peaches.

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter (January–February in zone 9) in well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.5), fertile loam in full sun; Tropic Beauty requires only 150 chill hours and ripens earlier than Florida Prince, making it a valuable option for hot Gulf Coast climates where the growing season is long but chill hours are limited.

directions: Plant bare-root trees in late winter in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile loam in full sun; Tropic Beauty is a University of Florida-developed low-chill peach cultivar requiring only 150 chill hours — making it adapted to the warmest portions of zone 8b, all of zone 9, and zone 10 in protected locations. Tropic Beauty produces medium to large fruits with yellow flesh and good sweet peach flavor, ripening in late April to May in zone 9 — one of the earliest-ripening yellow-flesh peaches available and a particularly valuable trait in Gulf South climates where spring arrives early and summer heat quickly limits the harvest window. The tree has an upright, vigorous growth habit and adapts well to the pruning required to maintain an open center structure for air circulation in humid climates. Like all peaches in the Gulf South, Tropic Beauty requires preventive fungicide management (copper at dormancy, follow-up fungicides through bloom) for disease control in the humid climate. Self-fertile — produces without a pollinizer, though cross-pollination with another low-chill variety improves yield. Thin fruit aggressively to 6 inches after June drop for maximum fruit size and quality; unthinned trees produce many small fruits that are commercially worthless and exhaust the tree.

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.0), fertile loam in full sun; plant two different pear cultivars for cross-pollination (Hood, Kieffer, Ayers); Warren pear requires only moderate chill hours (600–800) and excels in zone 7–9.

directions: Plant bare-root trees in late winter in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile loam in full sun; Warren is an outstanding southern heirloom pear discovered in Hattiesburg, Mississippi, by T.O. Warren around 1960 — a chance seedling that produces buttery, very sweet, nearly grit-free fruit with the silky texture of a fine European dessert pear. This exceptional fruit quality, combined with good fire blight resistance (the most devastating pear disease in humid southern climates), has made Warren one of the most highly regarded pear varieties for the Gulf South in recent decades. Most European pears are highly susceptible to fire blight (Erwinia amylovora), the bacterial disease that kills branches and causes heavy losses in hot, humid climates — Warren's relative resistance makes it a practical choice where standard European pears like Bartlett routinely fail. The medium-to-large, greenish-yellow fruits ripen in August in zone 9 and require off-tree ripening (store at room temperature after harvest) to reach their full, buttery sweetness. Plant with Hood or Kieffer for cross-pollination. Warren is not always easy to find at nurseries but is available from regional southern specialty nurseries and bare-root mail-order sources.

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.0), fertile loam in full sun; Flordahome requires only 150–200 chill hours and is the best pear choice for zone 9b–10 and the warmest portions of zone 9a where other pear varieties fail to produce consistently.

directions: Plant bare-root trees in late winter in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile loam in full sun; Flordahome is a University of Florida-developed pear cultivar requiring only 150–200 chill hours — the lowest chill requirement of any commonly available pear cultivar, making it the primary choice for zone 9b, zone 10, and any warm-winter location where standard pear varieties (which require 600–1000+ chill hours) consistently fail to bloom and fruit adequately. Flordahome produces medium-sized, yellow-green, round fruits with mild, sweet flesh that ripens in early summer — often in June in zone 9 — earlier than most other pear varieties. The fruit quality is pleasant but not as buttery or complex as high-chill European pears; it is best enjoyed fresh at peak ripeness or processed into preserves and sauces. Plant with Hood or Orient pear for cross-pollination — Flordahome is not self-fertile and requires a pollinizer. Fire blight resistance is moderate; preventive copper sprays at bloom reduce fire blight incidence in humid Gulf South climates. Flordahome trees grow vigorously in zone 9 and may need pruning to maintain manageable size — prune annually during dormancy to develop and maintain an open-center structure.

planting.note: Plant container-grown or bare-root trees in late winter to early spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.0), fertile soil in full sun; Jiro is self-fertile and does not require a pollinizer, though production typically improves with a nearby pollinizer variety; requires 200–400 chill hours.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; Jiro is the most widely grown non-astringent Japanese persimmon cultivar in California, Asia, and the Gulf South — producing large, slightly flattened, bright orange-red fruits that can be eaten crisp, firm, and crunchy directly from the tree without waiting for softening, making them much more approachable than astringent persimmons (like Hachiya) that must be fully soft before eating. The non-astringent quality means Jiro persimmons are eaten like apples — sliced, eaten fresh, added to salads, or simply eaten out of hand with no waiting for ripeness. The sweet, mild, honey-like flavor is pleasant and distinctive. In zone 9, Jiro ripens in October–November, adding late-season ornamental color when the bright orange fruits hang on the increasingly bare branches after leaf drop — a striking autumn display that has made ornamental persimmons popular landscape trees. The tree is naturally well-shaped and attractive with excellent fall foliage color. Jiro requires very little care once established — it is drought-tolerant, highly pest-resistant, and adapts to clay soils that challenge many fruit trees. One of the most productive and reliable fruit trees for Gulf South landscapes.
